coredata.dc:title: Red emission nitrogen and zinc co-doped carbon dots as fluorescent sensor for reversible detection of peroxynitrite in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Meng-Jie
Wei, Na
Pang, Lan-Fang
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), including ONOO-, H2O2, ClO-, etc, are inevitable products of aerobic metabolism. They involved in physiological processes and have concentration-dependent influences. Furthermore, their intracellular contents are always in a state of flux. Since then, the reversible and dynamic monitoring of ROS is of great significance. Till now, only a few nano sensors could achieve reversible detection of ROS, and their fluorescence wavelengths are short. Herein, we developed N, Zn co-doped CDs (NZnCDs) with red fluorescence for selective and reversible detection of ONOO-. The NZnCDs possess catechol residues on their surface. The reversible transition of quinone/phenol and their different effect on fluorescence of the CDs lay the foundation of the redox reversible sensor. With emission wavelength of 619 nm and quantum yield of 0.21, NZnCDs can selectively respond to ONOO-, giving a good linearity in 2–40 μM, and can be efficiently recovered by GSH. Finally, the proposed rare redox reversible CDs have been successfully applied for the imaging of ONOO-/GSH in MCF-7 cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reactive oxygen species
Peroxynitrite
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Redox
Reversible

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Redox cycle of NZnCDs with the regulation of ONOO- and GSH
The optical properties and stability of NZnCDs
Fluorescence detection of ONOO- with NZnCDs
Possible response mechanism of NZnCDs to ONOO- and GSH
Fluorescence imaging of ONOO-/GSH in living cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21777126 and 32070398 , Beijing, China). The authors thank the Large-Scale Instrument and Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University for the support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM imagine of the NZnCDs. (B) Size distribution of the NZnCDs. (C) XRD patterns of the NZnCDs.
(A) Full scan XPS spectrum of NZnCDs. (B-E) High resolution spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s and Zn2p.
FTIR spectra of NZnCDs (black line), NZnCDs + ONOO− (red line), and NZnCDs + ONOO− + β-mercaptoethanol (ME, blue line).
(A) UV-Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of NZnCDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of NZnCDs under different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence intensity of NZnCDs in phosphate buffer solutions with different pH (pH 2–12).
(A) Effect of pH on fluorescence quenching efficiency. (B) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of NZnCDs in presence of 50 μM ONOO- at 37 °C.

(A) Selectivity of NZnCDs to ONOO-. F0: Initial fluorescence intensity of NZnCDs without any analytes; F: Fluorescence intensity of NZnCDs with different analytes (50 μM for ONOO-, and 500 μM for all the other analytes). (0) NZnCDs, (1−23) NZnCDs with (1) ONOO-, (2) H2O2, (3) ClO-, (4) •OH, (5) O2 -, (6) t-BuOOH, (7) NO, (8) NO2 -, (9) NO3 -, (10) H2S, (11) Cys, (12) Hcy, (13) GSH, (14) K+, (15) Na+, (16) Cu2+, (17) Zn2+, (18) Ca2+, (19) Mg2+, (20) Hg2+, (21) Fe2+, (22) Fe3+and (23) Al3+. (B) Fluorescence spectra of NZnCDs after addition of different concentrations of ONOO- (Ex = 545 nm, Slit width: 5 nm/5 nm). (C) A linear relationship of quenching efficiency of NZnCDs at 619 nm versus ONOO- concentrations from 2 to 40 μM. F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of NZnCDs in the absence and presence of ONOO- respectively. (D) Fluorescence recovery behaviors of the assay with different antioxidants. (0) NZnCDs, (1) NZnCDs with 50 μM ONOO-. (2−7) NZnCDs were oxidized by 50 μM ONOO- and treated with different antioxidants for 15 min: (2) GSH, (3) Cys, (4) Hcy, (5) H2S, (6) AA, (7) Glu, 100 μM for GSH and 500 μM for others. (E) Fluorescence spectra of oxidized NZnCDs with different concentrations of GSH (Ex = 545 nm, Slit width: 5 nm/5 nm). (F) Fluorescence behaviors of NZnCDs in redox cycles.

(A) Fluorescence intensity decay curves of NZnCDs with (black) and without (red) 50 μM ONOO-. (B) UV-Vis absorption spectra of NZnCDs (black line), NZnCDs + ONOO− (red line), NZnCDs + ONOO− + GSH (blue line), and NZnCDs + ONOO− + β-mercaptoethanol (ME, amaranthine line).

Confocal fluorescence imagines of MCF-7 cells. (A-C) Cells incubated with NZnCDs (100 μg/mL) for 4 h. (D-F) Cells incubated with NZnCDs (100 μg/mL) for 4 h and treated with 0.3 mM NEM (5 min) and 50 μM ONOO- (10 min). (G-I) Cells incubated with NZnCDs (100 μg/mL) for 4 h and treated with 0.3 mM NEM (5 min), 50 μM ONOO- (10 min) and 100 μM GSH (30 min) in sequence. Air immersion lens: 40×. Excitation channel wavelength: 561 nm. Emission channel: 600 nm (bandwidth: 50 nm).

head.ce:title: Red emission nitrogen and zinc co-doped carbon dots as fluorescent sensor for reversible detection of peroxynitrite in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), including ONOO-, H2O2, ClO-, etc, are inevitable products of aerobic metabolism. They involved in physiological processes and have concentration-dependent influences. Furthermore, their intracellular contents are always in a state of flux. Since then, the reversible and dynamic monitoring of ROS is of great significance. Till now, only a few nano sensors could achieve reversible detection of ROS, and their fluorescence wavelengths are short. Herein, we developed N, Zn co-doped CDs (NZnCDs) with red fluorescence for selective and reversible detection of ONOO-. The NZnCDs possess catechol residues on their surface. The reversible transition of quinone/phenol and their different effect on fluorescence of the CDs lay the foundation of the redox reversible sensor. With emission wavelength of 619 nm and quantum yield of 0.21, NZnCDs can selectively respond to ONOO-, giving a good linearity in 2–40 μM, and can be efficiently recovered by GSH. Finally, the proposed rare redox reversible CDs have been successfully applied for the imaging of ONOO-/GSH in MCF-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are generated from the aerobic metabolism in biological systems [1], featured with high reactivity and rapid diffusion [2]. Their intracellular contents are regulated by an elaborate system, consisting of antioxidant enzymes, biothiols, vitamins and flavonoids, etc [3,4]. ROS play crucial roles in physiological processes and have double-edged characters. A well-controlled level of ROS is indispensable in homeostasis regulation and cell signaling [5]. However, overproduced ROS can cause oxidative stress, then bring irreparable damage to DNA, lipids, proteins and membranes [6]. The aberrant ROS level also relate to cancer [7], inflammation [8], diabetes, neurodegeneration and other diseases [9]. Since the intracellular ROS contents are always in a state of flux, reversible and dynamic monitoring of ROS is important for the investigation of the cellular redox state.

Fluorescence technology is a promising approach for dynamic monitoring in biosystems, owing to its superiority in non-invasive real-time imaging, including high sensitivity, quick response, high imaging contrast and temporal-spatial resolution [10–13]. Small organic molecule fluorescent probes for reversible detection of ROS have been developed, covering the whole wavelength from UV to NIR. However, all of them require complex synthesis procedure [14–19].

Carbon dots have drawn more interests because of their unique traits, including simple preparation procedures, low cost, high water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and outstanding photostability [20–23]. They have become powerful candidates for fluorescent detection and bioimaging. CDs have been successfully used for the detection of ROS, but most of them exhibit irreversible response [24–27]. Till now, only a few CDs have been developed for reversible fluorescence imaging of ROS. CDs synthesized from hyaluronic acid and cationic conjugated polymers can reversibly respond to NaClO and GSH at 425 nm [28]. Qian et. al. designed a redox-controlled fluorescent carbon dots based on reversible disulfide bonds [29]. The fluorescence at 523 nm can be quenched by H2O2 and recovered by some reducers like glutathione, sodium dithionite, penicillamine and dithiothreitol. Selenium is another important redox responsive component using in probes. The first Se-CQDs was reported in 2017 [30]. Its maximum emission located at 490 nm. Its fluorescence intensity decreased when ROS levels elevated in cells. Another selenium doped CD can respond to hydroxyl radical (•OH) and glutathione through reversible transition of CSe and SeSe groups [31]. Then CDs with maximum emission at 545 nm has been applied in the reversible detection of hypochlorite and ascorbic acid via oxidation and reduction of the amino groups on the surface [32]. However, to our best knowledge, the signals of all these reported CDs are in the violet or green channels, and the background autofluorescence interference, limited tissue penetration may confine their application in bioimaging. Therefore, redox-reversible fluorescent nano switches for ROS with long emission wavelength still need exploitation.

In this work, we design a redox-reversible fluorescent nano switch based on the redox transformation between phenol and quinone, which can cause significant change in fluorescence intensity. We tried to extend the emission wavelength by constructing large conjugate structures through aromatic diamine and diphenol. Thus, NZnCDs with red fluorescence emission at 619 nm (QY = 21%) were prepared via a one-pot solvothermal method taking 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid, catechol and zinc chloride as precursors. The prepared CDs can respond to ONOO- selectively and reversibly in the presence of GSH, and have been successfully applied to monitor ONOO-/GSH in MCF-7 cells. Scheme 1.

3,4-diaminobenzoic acid and biothiols were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Catechol, ethanol, zinc chloride and other inorganic salts were gained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Reactive oxygen species were prepared according to reported methods [33,34]. All the reagents and chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water produced by purification system (Ulupure, China) was used throughout the experiments.

CDs were prepared through a one-pot solvothermal method. At first, 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid (50 mg), catechol (50 mg) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 25 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol. Then the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooled to room temperature naturally, the obtained dark purple suspension was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles. Then the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon microporous membrane. The filtrate was collected and dialyzed through a membrane, which has a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da, for 12 h toward water to remove ethanol and unreacted precusors. Finally, the resulting solution was freeze-dried to obtain NZnCDs as a dark purple solid. The obtained CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The detection of peroxynitrite was performed in phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4). Different volumes of ONOO- stock solution was added into NZnCDs solution (100 μg/mL). The mixture was shaken thoroughly. After incubated at 37 ℃ for 2 min, the fluorescence spectra were measured at 619 nm with an excitation wavelength of 545 nm. To estimate the selectivity of NZnCDs, 500 μM of different potential interferences were added into the CDs solutions respectively, instead of ONOO-, including H2O2, ClO-, •OH, O2 -, t-BuOOH, NO, NO2 -, NO3 -, H2S, Cys, Hcy, GSH, K+, Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+and Al3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Redox cycle of NZnCDs with the regulation of ONOO- and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: To appraise the reversibility of the NZnCDs, a serious of tests were conducted in phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4). The NZnCDs solutions reacted with 50 μM ONOO- first, and then different volumes of GSH stock solution were added into the mixtures to make GSH final concentrations ranging in 0–100 μM. After vortex, the mixtures were incubated at 37 ℃ for 15 min. Corresponding fluorescence spectra were collected under the same conditions of the ONOO- detection (Section 2.3). To assess the selectivity of the recovery process, 500 μM of five other antioxidants co-existing in biosystem were added into the system severally, instead of GSH, including cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), ascorbic acid (AA) and glucose (Glu). When the dosage of GSH was optimized, a second dose of ONOO- and GSH were added sequentially to explore the performance of the assay in the redox cycle regulated by ONOO- and GSH.

Catechol and 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid were selected, because they could form large conjugated sp2-domains, which may cause significant red-shift in the fluorescence of CDs [35,36]. The quinone/phenol redox couple has been used in previous designs of fluorescent switches. They can realize mutual transformation during redox reaction via electron transfer. Quinone moiety can efficiently quench the fluorescence of most fluorophores, while phenol has little influence on the fluorescence [37–39]. According to this theory, we presumed that catechol could bring phenol groups to the surface of the CDs and constructed a reversible redox fluorescent sensor. However, CDs obtained from those two precursors can be quenched by several metal ions (NCDs in Fig. S1). It has been reported that metal-doped CDs show metal-ion-tolerance and better selectivity [40]. Compared with other metals, zinc has less toxicity [41] and negligible influence on the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S1). Zinc chloride has been used to both enhance the fluorescence of CDs and make red-shift of the emission wavelength [42,43]. Thus, zinc chloride was chosen to improve the performance of the CDs.

The TEM image in Fig. 1 A indicates that the as-prepared NZnCDs are well-dispersed nanospheres. Taking 150 particles into account, the size distribution of NZnCDs is in the range of 1.25–5.75 nm, with an average diameter of 3.51 nm (Fig. 1B). The particles have no clear crystal lattice, indicating an amorphous structure, which has been further confirmed by XRD analysis. Like most reported CDs, a broad diffraction peak around 22° can be seen in the XRD patterns of the NZnCDs (Fig. 1 C).

The element components and surface composition of NZnCDs were illustrated by XPS and FTIR. The XPS full scan spectrum in Fig. 2 A reveals five typical peaks: C1s (285.0 eV), N1s (398.4 eV), O1s (532.0 eV), and Zn2p (1021.8 and 1044.8 eV). C1s spectrum can be divided into four subpeaks corresponding to CC (285.7 eV), CC (284.7 eV), CO (288.9 eV), C-O/C-N (286.9 eV). The three fitted peaks in N1s spectrum are assigned to pyrrolic N (399.5 eV), pyridinic N (400.2 eV) and graphitic N (401 eV) respectively. The three subpeaks in the O1s spectrum indicate the presence of CO (531.7 eV), COC (531.4 eV) and COH (533 eV). The two peaks in Zn2p spectrum are attribute to Zn2p1 (1023 eV) and Zn2p3 (1045 eV) respectively.

The black line in Fig. 3 displays the FTIR spectrum of NZnCDs. Peaks at 3446 cm−1 and 3329 cm−1 come from stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups (COH) in catechol. The stretching vibration band of benzene skeleton can be found at 1605 cm−1. Peaks at 1514 cm−1 and 1470 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of carboxyl groups. The peak at 1358 cm−1 is from the bending vibration of the phenolic hydroxyl group.
Both XPS and FTIR results proved that there are residues of catechol unit on the surface of NZnCDs. They enhanced the water solubility of the CDs and provided reaction sites at the same time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties and stability of NZnCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of NZnCDs were investigated by UV-Vis and fluorescence spectral analysis. As shown in Fig. 4A, NZnCDs present an absorption peak at 541 nm, which might be assigned to the n-π* transition of CN group and result in strong fluorescence emission [44]. Under the optimized excitation wavelength at 545 nm, the maximum emission can be observed at 619 nm with a shoulder peak at 672 nm. As revealed in Fig. 4B, the wavelength of the emission peaks had no obvious change while the excitation light shifted from 465 to 605 nm. The excitation-independent fluorescence behavior indicates that the surface of the CDs is well-passivated and uniform [45]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CDs is calculated to be 21%, taking rhodamine B (QY = 0.97, at 535 nm in ethanol) as standard.

To estimate the stability of NZnCDs, a series of experiments were carried out, including pH, ionic strength and photo irradiation. The fluorescence of the CDs was quenched at pH 2, and increased significantly as pH raised to 4. Then it became relatively stable in the pH range of 4–12. ( Fig. 5) And there was no obvious change in fluorescence intensity of the CDs, when NaCl concentration raised from 0 to 300 mM in the buffer solutions (Fig. S2). Similar phenomena can be seen after 12 h continuous irradiation with a 100 W incandescent lamp at 30 cm (Fig. S3).

A serious of experiments were carried out to investigate the effects of pH and reaction time on quenching efficiency. High quenching efficiency has been found at pH range of 7–12 ( Fig. 6A), which is suitable for detection and imaging in biological system. Considering the application in biosystem, reaction temperature was set at 37 ℃. Then the response time of NZnCDs toward peroxynitrite was examined. After the addition of ONOO-, the fluorescence of NZnCDs solution quenched rapidly and reached a platform within 2 min (Fig. 6B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ONOO- with NZnCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of NZnCDs is essential for their application in complex biological samples. Therefore, the CDs’ responses toward potential interferences were explored, including ROS/RNS (H2O2, ClO-, •OH, O2 -, t-BuOOH, NO, NO2 -, NO3 -), hydrogen sulfide, biothiols (Cys, Hcy, GSH), and metal ions (K+, Na+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+). As revealed in Fig. 7A, the examined interfering substances have little influence on the fluorescence intensity of NZnCDs.

For the sensitivity experiments, different volumes of ONOO- stock solution were added into the NZnCDs solution and incubated at 37 ℃ for 2 min in phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4). The fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreased while the ONOO- concentration raised from 0 to 50 μM (Fig. 7B). An excellent linearity between the quenching ratio and ONOO- concentration was obtained within 2–40 μM (Fig. 7C). The linear equation is (F0 - F) / F0 = 0.01791c(ONOO-) + 0.08404 (R2 = 0.9996), where F0 and F represent fluorescence intensities of NZnCDs in the absence and presence of ONOO- respectively. The detection limit for ONOO- was calculated to be 0.6 μM (S / N = 3). (A comparison with other ONOO- detection methods based on fluorescent CDs is given in Table S1.).

Some antioxidants, including GSH, Cys, Hcy, ascorbic acid (AA) and glucose (Glu), were added into the assay. As depicted in Fig. 7D, the assay exhibits a specific response to GSH. The fluorescence quenched by 50 μM ONOO- was restored within 15 min after adding 2 equivalents of GSH (Fig. S4). Moreover, after the reversible cycle has been repeated for three times, the fluorescence intensity of the assay still measured 87% of the initial intensity (Fig. 7E). When the interference from the volume increased in the cycles was eliminated by normalization, the recovery rate was calculated to be 95% (Fig. S5). The other two biothiols (Cys and Hcy) can also restore fluorescence of the oxidized NZnCDs to some extent, but their recovery efficiencies are far lower than that of GSH (Fig. S6). Considering contents of the thiols in living cells, GSH was chosen as the reductant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of NZnCDs to ONOO- and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Reaction mechanisms of NZnCDs with ONOO- and GSH were explored. The fluorescence intensity decay curves are in Fig. 8A. The fluorescence lifetime of NZnCDs was calculated to be 1.18 ns and slightly changed to 1.14 ns in the presence of ONOO-. This result indicates that a static quenching process is involved in the reaction of CDs and ONOO- [46]. New peak in UV-Vis spectrum after the oxidation of ONOO- (Fig. 8B, red line) also corroborated that new groups appear on the NZnCDs and a static quenching process has happened. After the addition of GSH and β-mercaptoethanol (ME), an absorption peak appeared around 541 nm again (Fig. 8B, blue line and amaranthine line), which also indicates the reversibility of the redox process. The red line in FTIR spectra (Fig. 3) was obtained after addition of excess ONOO-. The broad peak at 3396 cm−1 is significantly different from the peaks of NZnCDs in the black line, which indicates the catechol residuals on the surface of NZnCDs have reacted with ONOO-. Some changes occurred in the peaks between 1514 and 850 cm−1 also indicate the decrease of hydroxyls in the oxidation. In the investigation of the reduction process, peaks of excess GSH masked the feature peaks of NZnCDs in FTIR spectra. And because of the similar molecular weights and particle sizes, it is hard to remove GSH from the mixture. Thus, we used β-mercaptoethanol (ME), a volatile thiol, as reductant in FTIR analysis. (Fig. S7 shows the fluorescence of the assay restored by ME.) The blue line in Fig. 3 shows that the quinone groups on the surface of the CDs are successfully reduced to phenol, after addition of the thiol. The FTIR spectra prove that the reversible responses to ONOO-and thiols can be mainly attributed to the redox of the catechol residues on the surface of NZnCDs. Then, Zeta-potential of the NZnCDs was tested to be −10.27 mV, which decreased to −20.43 mV with addition of ONOO- and increased to −10.79 mV by GSH. Similar results were obtained in parallel experiments when β-mercaptoethanol was taken as the reductant. The Zeta-potential were reduced from −11.4 mV to −20.4 mV, when NZnCDs reacted with ONOO-, then increased to −11.5 mV after the addition of β-mercaptoethanol These consequences demonstrate that during their reactions with ONOO- and GSH, reversible changes have taken place on the surface of the NZnCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of ONOO-/GSH in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity of NZnCDs was evaluated by MTT assay with MCF-7 cell line. As described in Fig. S8, more than 90% cells remained alive after incubation with 300 μg/mL NZnCDs for 18 h. The results indicate that NZnCDs are safe to cells in their working concentration. As depicted in Fig. 9, after incubated with NZnCDs, a visible fluorescence signal could be observed in the red channel. The fluorescence intensity decreased when 50 μM ONOO- was added, and then recovered by two equivalents of GSH. These results confirm that NZnCDs are capable of sensing redox cycles through reversible fluorescence responses in living cells.

In conclusion, we have developed a kind of red emission NZnCDs for the reversible detection of ONOO-. The CDs are prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal treatment toward 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid, catechol and zinc chloride. They exhibit good optical properties, high selectivity, and excellent biocompatibility, and have been successfully applied in the imaging of ONOO-/GSH in MCF-7 cells. Their response mechanisms toward ONOO- and GSH have been studied in detail. The fluorescence switch based on the reversible transformation between phenol and quinone may provide a new ideal for the design of fluorescent reversible nano probe.

Meng-Jie Fu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Software. Na Wei: Software. Lan-Fang Pang: Software. Xiao-Feng Guo: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Hong Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21777126 and 32070398, Beijing, China). The authors thank the Large-Scale Instrument and Equipment Sharing Foundation of Wuhan University for the support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130939.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Meng-Jie Fu is a doctoral student in the College of Chemistry and Molecular Science, Wuhan University. Her research interest is focused on synthesis and applications of fluorescent nanoprobes.
Na Wei is a doctoral student of College of Chemistry and Molecular Science, Wuhan University.
Lan-Fang Pang is a doctoral student of College of Chemistry and Molecular Science, Wuhan University.

Xiao-Feng Guo had a bachelor’s degree in chemistry (2004) from Wuhan University in Wuhan, China. In 2004 he joined the Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine in Wuhan University where he obtained his Ph.D. in analytical chemistry in 2009. His main research interests are focused on computational chemistry, small-molecule fluorescent probe and fluorescent nanoprobes.

Hong Wang studied chemistry at Wuhan University and completed her bachelor studies in the college of chemistry and molecular science in 1988. She obtained her Ph.D. at Wuhan University in Wuhan, China, in 1994. She has been working for the molecular probe group in the College of Chemistry and Molecular Science in Wuhan University since 1996. Now, she is the leader of molecular probe group.
